History
The approach to obtaining the history of children varies based on 
their age and developmental level. As much as possible, children 
should be included in the discussion of their health. When the 
history is obtained from the parent or caregiver, as with very 
young children, the adult’s perception of their child’s function 
and health is often inconsistent with the child’s perception. On 
the initial visit to a pediatric physiatrist, it is helpful to the child 
and family to summarize the reason for the referral because they 
may not know why they have been referred. They may have lim -
ited understanding of what a pediatric physiatrist is and what they 
can expect from the visit. A brief description of what the evalua -
tion will entail, the focus on functional assessment, and what the 
physiatrist can offer the child and family helps set the tone and 
alleviate anxiety. Asking the child and family what they feel is the 
underlying cause or problem also assists in evaluating the family’s 
current understanding of the child’s medical condition and deter-
mining what concerns are important to the family.
The World Health Organization defines adolescence as the 
phase of life between childhood and adulthood, from ages 10 to  
19 years. This marks a time of unique and significant psychologi-
cal, cognitive, social, and physical developmental changes. With 
adolescents, it is important to create a safe space where they can 
freely express themselves. Therefore it may be beneficial to have the  
parent or caregiver leave the examination area to allow them to 
provide additional history or ask questions that they may be  
uncomfortable doing in the presence of family. Throughout the 
adolescent interview, it is essential to express empathy, promote their 
autonomy, empower them to make any needed changes, and main-
tain confidentiality. However, adolescents should be made aware  
of specific instances when private information may be disclosed, 
such as suicidal or homicidal ideation or acts, life-threatening drug 
misuse, or an eating disorder causing bodily harm.15
Birth History
A thorough history includes identifying any issues during preg -
nancy, during labor, and at delivery. Maternal complications dur-
ing pregnancy, such as seizures, febrile illnesses, hypertension, or 
hyperglycemia, should be evaluated. Any medications, supple -
ments, alcohol, tobacco, or other substances that the mother used 
during the pregnancy should be reviewed for potential impact on 
the fetus. Infants exposed to alcohol or cocaine, for example, often 
have significant cognitive, behavioral, and motoric impairments. 
The duration of gestation, presence of multiples, and presentation 
at birth are also important factors. Premature delivery and multi -
ples are known risk factors for cerebral palsy. 1,45 The examiner 
should ask if the mother received prenatal care. If a mother did not 
receive prenatal care, then she may not have taken additional folate 
to decrease the risk for spinal dysraphisms. 33 The family should  
be asked if there was a decrease in fetal movements noted at any 
time during the pregnancy that could indicate a neuromuscular 
problem such as spinal muscular atrophy. Ask about accidents or 
illnesses during pregnancy in the mother or accidents/illness in 
infancy for the child.
The child’s birth weight, length, and Apgar scores should be 
noted. Higher birth weights with vaginal delivery may lead to a 
• An obligatory reflex is never normal, and children should be 
evaluated for central nervous system disturbance.
• Children’s musculoskeletal and neurologic exams are expected 
to change over time, most rapidly within the first few years  
of life.
• Children and adolescents often present with musculoskeletal 
complaints that can have a broad differential diagnosis, and it is 
important to differentiate between inflammatory and mechanical 
joint problems.
• The pediatric history and physical examination must be tailored 
to each child and family based off of developmental level,  
diagnoses, and goals of patient and family.
• The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s “Learn the 
Signs. Act Early” program revised its developmental surveillance 
checklists or milestones in 2022.
• A wide range of developmental tests are available for children 
based on age, skills, and diagnoses and are useful in approaching 
assessment in a uniform way.
• Careful observation of the child at play or interacting with  
others can provide useful information for a pediatric exam.
SUMMARY PEARLS

SECTION 1  Evaluation
fractured clavicle and brachial plexus injury. The Apgar score 
consists of five components, activity, pulse, grimace, appearance, 
and respiration, each scored from 0 to 2 and recorded at 1 min -
ute, 5 minutes, and 9 minutes, with a score of 7 to 10 considered 
normal. Postnatal complications, such as hyperbilirubinemia, 
retinopathy of prematurity, respiratory difficulties, feeding diffi -
culties, and duration of respiratory support, may provide clues to 
underlying pathology and functional impact. Complications  
with previous pregnancies, such as stillbirth, miscarriages, or fetal 
anomalies, should be recorded.
History of Presenting Problem
The physician should determine the onset of the current problem 
and any associated factors. It should be noted if the symptoms are 
worsening, static, or improving. It is important to determine 
which diagnostic tests have been performed and the treatments 
that have been initiated. Questions regarding the child’s tempera-
ment and personality can provide insight into the child’s readiness 
to participate in therapies and which approaches may be most 
helpful. The child’s medical history should be reviewed for any 
similar problems and any significant illnesses, hospitalizations, 
surgeries, procedures, or previous trauma. Medications should be 
reviewed for any possible side effects, particularly on the nervous 
and/or muscular systems. Allergies should also be reviewed, in -
cluding any feeding intolerances. A history of early allergies with 
different formulas may indicate a feeding difficulty rather than  
a true allergy. Immunization status is important to determine 
because of the risk for disorders such as tetanus.
Developmental History
Developmental history is one of the most important aspects of 
pediatric history. Illnesses, injuries, and different disease processes 
can have a profound impact on the attainment of developmental 
milestones, with delays noted in gross motor, fine motor, speech 
and language, and/or psychosocial areas. A thorough understand-
ing of the developmental milestones ( Table 2.1) and the age at 
which the child attained them can assist with diagnosis and treat-
ment protocols.
56 If the delays are primarily motoric in nature, 
then a neuromuscular disorder is more likely. If the delays are 
noted in speech and language skills, then further assessment of the 
child’s hearing is warranted. Parents should be asked if there is a 
history of recurrent ear infections. Infants with hearing loss start 
to fall behind after 6 to 8 months of age and may present with 
decreased babbling.

A discussion of the developmental milestones is also helpful 
in educating the family regarding what children should be doing 
and what skills they should be working on. As an example, 
families are often focused on children walking when they need 
to take a step back and work on sitting independently or stand -
ing balance. It should also be emphasized that there is a wide age 
range of normal for the attainment of certain skills, and families 
may notice that their child progresses at different rates within 
the different areas. Note that in prematurity, corrected age (actual 
gestational age in weeks 2 weeks preterm 5 corrected age) is 
used in assessing developmental milestones up to 2 years of 
age. In children with known or severe developmental delays, 
families often prefer to focus on what their child can do, rather 
than focus on what they cannot do. Reframing questions to 
celebrate the accomplishments of these children can help build 
trust and rapport with their families.
Family History
The family history should include any history of early stroke, 
early myocardial infarction, peripheral neuropathy, joint or tis-
sue abnormalities, myopathies, and bony abnormalities. Gait 
abnormalities or developmental delays should be investigated 
through multiple generations. Certain disease processes, such as 
myotonic dystrophy, will present in milder forms in previous 
generations and may be missed until a more severely affected 
family member presents for medical evaluation. If a genetic 
disorder is suspected, then the child and family should be re -
ferred for genetic testing. Advances in whole exome sequencing 
and whole genome sequencing have improved the presumptive 
diagnostic rate of neurodevelopmental disorders and provided 
guidance in further screening and medication management.
38 
This can help with planning for future pregnancies and counsel -
ing to the extended family. It is important to note, however, that 
genetic abnormalities due to nucleotide expansions or repeats, 
such as myotonic dystrophy and Friedreich ataxia, may not be 
picked up on whole exome or genome sequencing and may 
require additional specialized testing.
Social and Educational History
The examiner should inquire about the child’s environment, 
including who lives with the child in the home, the primary 
and secondary caregivers, the primary language spoken in the 
home, and the layout of the home. Accessibility is assessed by 
determining the number of stairs to enter the home, the levels 
of the home, and where the child’s bedroom and bathroom are 
located. It is important to also evaluate the child’s peer group 
and interactions with peers. Extracurricular activities can give 
insight into a child’s social skills and personality. The child’s 
current educational history should include grade level, pres -
ence of an individualized education or 504 plan for additional 
education supports, any failed or repeated grade levels, and 
whether the child ever received early intervention services. 
Early intervention services evaluate and support children, ages 
0 to 36 months, who are at risk for delays or have delays in one 
or more areas (gross motor, fine motor, language).
34 It is ben-
eficial to provide families with information on how to obtain 
and manage special education services among the good re -
sources that are available.
54,55 A nationally and internationally 
validated tool to assess adolescent psychosocial history is the 
HEEADSSS method, which focuses on assessment of the 
home environment, education and employment, eating, peer-
related activities, drugs, sexuality, suicide/depression, and 
safety from injury and violence ( Table 2.2).
20,21,28 Other  
useful screening tools include CRAFFT (car, relax, alone,  
forget, friends, trouble), which is a brief, validated, office-
friendly screening test useful in initially assessing adolescent 
(age 12–26 years) substance use,
28 and the Ask Suicide-Screening 
Questions tool, which is a set of four screening questions to 
identify suicide risk. A “yes” response to one or more of the 
questions identified 97% of youth (age 10 to 21 years) at risk

SECTION 1  Evaluation

part of trauma assessment after motor vehicle collisions involv -
ing adolescents. Updated versions of the CRAFFT include 
vaping and edibles for marijuana use and a nicotine checklist 
for tobacco and nicotine use are available.
Physical Examination
There is no standardized approach to the examination of the pediat-
ric patients.2,27 It should be tailored to the age and developmental 
level of each child. The examination commences with the first intro-
ductions. As the examiner is obtaining the history, observation of the 
child can provide a great deal of information about their social skills, 
language, motor movements, and personality. Before touching, it is 
helpful to develop a rapport by playing or talking with each child. A 
penlight, a badge on a retractable holder, or bubbles are easy tools to 
engage children and test their visual fixation, reach, grasp, and re-
lease at the same time. Knowledge of popular cartoon or movie 
characters, popular athletes/teams, or singers can be very helpful in 
engaging children. Very young children are typically most comfort-
able on their parent’s lap during the examination, where they feel 
safe. Infants are usually tolerant of gentle physical handling by a 
stranger until about 9 months of age, when stranger anxiety devel-
ops. Older children and adolescents can easily be evaluated on the 
examination table, although autism and other cognitive impair -
ments can require accommodations in the physical exam.
Growth
Height and weight should be plotted and monitored as the child 
grows. The average full-term newborn measures 50 cm in length; 
height increases 50% by age 1 year and doubles by 4 years of 
age.24 A child’s adult height can be estimated by doubling the 
height at age 2 years. Short stature may be seen with T urner and 
Down syndromes. Growth may be arrested early because of 
precocious puberty with premature closure of the growth plates. 
Precocious puberty, defined as the onset of puberty in females 
younger than 8 years and males younger than 9 years of age, 51  
is commonly seen in cerebral palsy, other brain injuries, and 
spina bifida.
The average full-term newborn in the United States weighs 
3400 g. It is normal for the infant to initially lose weight, but this 
is regained quickly, and by 5 months of age the weight should 
have doubled. By 1 year, the child’s birth weight should have tri-
pled. Any deviation off the child’s expected growth curve should 
be evaluated closely. Decreases in height velocity or weight loss 
may be associated with poor nutrition or malabsorption. Signifi -
cant increases may indicate a pituitary tumor, metabolic disorder, 
or poor diet. Childhood obesity has reached epidemic propor -
tions in the United States, is considered a health care emergency, 
and is a particular issue in many children with disabilities. 
Growth charts are available for specific genetic syndromes (e.g., 
T urner, Williams, Down syndromes), the cerebral palsy popula -
tion (based on their gross motor functional classification), and the 
myelomeningocele population.
In evaluating children, a head circumference should be checked 
and monitored serially for the first several years. The average head 
circumference at birth is 35 cm and increases to 47 cm by 1 year 
of age.24 If the child has macrocephaly, defined as a head circum-
ference greater than 2 standard deviations above the mean, a 
quick inspection of the parents’ head sizes may help to differenti-
ate between a familial trait and hydrocephalus or some type of

mass. Hydrocephalus may be present at birth or develop over 
time. It is commonly seen after closure of a myelomeningocele 
defect. Microcephaly is a head circumference greater than 2 stan-
dard deviations below the mean and indicates that the brain has 
not fully formed, as in anencephaly, or that growth has been  
arrested because of some sort of insult, such as anoxia or infection. 
It may also be seen in certain neurodegenerative disorders.
Inspection
On evaluating the child, initial inspection should assess the child’s 
general appearance, movements, engagement, and overall health. 
Close observations of any facial anomalies, joint abnormalities, or 
asymmetry of stature or sides should be noted. The presence of 
certain physical abnormalities is linked with some common syn -
dromes (Table 2.3).
Skin examinations may be significant for birthmarks such as 
port-wine stains, hyperpigmented areas such as café au lait spots 
(Fig. 2.3), axillary freckling, or acute changes such as the scalded 
skin appearance of Stevens-Johnson syndrome or the purpura of 
meningococcal infections. Port-wine stains in the distribution of 
the first branch of the trigeminal nerve are associated with Sturge-
Weber syndrome. A sacral dimple, skin lesions over the spine, and/
or a hairy tuft over the lumbar or sacral spine may indicate spina

SECTION 1  Evaluation
Evaluation of the head and neck should note cranial deformi-
ties, facial dysmorphisms, and asymmetries. With the institution 
of the “Back to Sleep” program in 1992, many children now 
present to their pediatricians with flattening of the occiput, 
brachycephaly, or plagiocephaly, which usually resolves when 
they begin to sit independently.
39 Epicanthal folds and palmar 
simian creases are well-known hallmarks of Down syndrome. 
Other conditions present with anomalies that include low-set 
ears, hypertelorism, wide-set eyes, and micrognathia. If the sclera 
of the eyes has a bluish tinge, then the examiner should consider 
osteogenesis imperfecta.
Musculoskeletal Assessment
The musculoskeletal assessment includes inspection and palpation of 
the bones, joints, and muscles. Any asymmetries in muscle mass or 
limb size should be noted. Lymphedema may present as swelling and 
increased growth in an upper and/or lower limb. Congenital ampu-
tations associated with amniotic band syndrome may include digi-
tal, transhumeral, transradial, or transtibial amputations. Apparent

The absence of the pectoral muscle and ipsilateral hand deformities 
with brachydactyly or syndactyly is described in Poland syndrome 
(Fig. 2.5). Palpation of the joints evaluates for tenderness, swelling, 
warmth, and synovial thickening. Both passive and active range of 
motion of all joints should be evaluated. Joint mobility will change 
during growth.
36,46 A full-term infant may lack as much as 25 de -
grees of elbow extension and 30 degrees of hip extension. A preterm 
infant, conversely, tends to have increased range of motion because 
of lower muscle tone. Loss of range of motion may be attributable 
to joint contracture from arthrogryposis, orthopedic conditions such 
as Klippel-Feil syndrome, spasticity, pain, inflammatory disorders

Connective tissue disorders, such as Marfan syndrome, will result in 
joint hypermobility and increased skin elasticity. Children who are 
anxious may not be as easy to examine, and the examiner will have 
to determine whether there is a volitional component affecting their 
range of motion.
The back and spine examination should focus on any bony 
abnormalities and any muscular asymmetries. Children should 
be examined in both sitting and standing positions because any 
leg length discrepancy may affect the spine and pelvis examina-
tion. Shoulder height, the position of the scapulae, the space 
between the trunk and the upper limbs, and the height of the 
pelvis should be evaluated. A rib hump and increased space  
between the trunk and upper limbs is noted in children  
with scoliosis ( Fig. 2.6). The curve should be evaluated by serial 
radiographs to determine the severity, flexibility, and progression

of the curve over time. Idiopathic scoliosis in adolescent females 
is the most common type of scoliosis, with a right thoracic curve

13 The rib hump can be further accentuated by having 
children bend forward. Other spinal abnormalities are listed in 
Table 2.4. A congenital elevation of the scapula or a Sprengel 
deformity (Fig. 2.7) is often seen in association with Klippel-Feil 
syndrome and cervical spine abnormalities.
Evaluation of gait is a common referral for the physiatrist. The 
lower limb examination may demonstrate range of motion and 
bony abnormalities. (In addition to evaluating their bodies, chil -
dren’s shoes should be assessed for abnormal wear patterns.) With 
femoral anteversion, the femur twists between the femoral neck 
and the femoral condyles, bringing the femoral neck more ante -
rior and causing increased internal rotation at the hip. The normal 
range of motion for internal rotation is 15 to 25 degrees. Children 
with femoral anteversion are often “W sitters,” and in walking, 
the patella is noted to be pointed inward. This is most pronounced 
at 4 to 6 years of age and often self-corrects by 8 to 10 years of

Neurologic Assessment
The neurologic assessment evaluates cranial nerve function, sen -
sory function, strength, movement, reflexes, coordination, balance, 
gait, and cognitive function. Sensory function may be difficult to 
evaluate in very young children; therefore a complete examination, 
including an assessment of light touch, proprioception, vibratory, 
pain, and temperature sensations, may be possible only in older 
children.
7 When infants and young children are being assessed, the 
examiner should use their nonverbal cues, such as looking toward 
touch stimulus or pulling the limb away when touched. Vibratory 
toys may be helpful in the evaluation.
Responses to visual stimuli may be attempted with a bright 
light or an interesting object. Infants will track an object to 
midline at 1 month of age and from side to side at 3 months of 
age. Perception of color develops by 2 months of age and depth 
perception by 3 to 5 months of age. Children should be evalu -
ated for any obvious ocular imbalance. Strabismus is a common 
finding in children with cerebral palsy. Patching, corrective 
lenses, and/or surgery is necessary at an early age to improve 
visual function.
30
Auditory evaluation is a part of routine newborn screening.  
It should be repeated with any child who demonstrates speech 
and language delays, articulation errors, inattentiveness to sound, 
history of recurrent ear infections, or history of brain injury.
6,35 In 
the infant, hearing can be evaluated by clapping or making a loud 
noise and watching for a startle (Moro) or blink response. Older 
children should respond to the rubbing of fingers close to their 
ears or using toys that make sounds. If abnormalities are noted, 
the ears should be checked for impaction or infection. If the ca-
nals are clear and there are no signs of infection, a comprehensive 
auditory evaluation is warranted.
Evaluation of primitive reflexes and postural responses is a help-
ful tool in checking the infant’s motor responses. These reflexes 
(Table 2.5) are controlled at the level of the brainstem and spinal 
cord.
14 As the central nervous system (CNS) matures, the reflexes 
are integrated and suppressed between 3 and 6 months of age. 
Obligatory primitive reflexes, or a reflex that a child cannot move 
out of, are never normal and indicate a CNS problem. Any asym-
metry of reflexes may reflect an underlying stroke or peripheral 
nerve disorder, such as a brachial plexus injury. Primitive  
reflexes are replaced by postural reactions ( Table 2.6) starting at 
approximately 2 months of age. These responses allow children to 
progress with motor development. Asymmetry in these responses

overhead, hold onto objects, creep, stand, and walk. The strength 
examination is affected by tone and must be interpreted carefully in 
children with hypertonia resulting from the inability to differentiate 
between voluntary and involuntary muscle activation. When a 
child’s muscle tone is being evaluated, it is important to remember 
that the tone will change during development. Premature infants 
often have lower muscle tone, which should improve with time. If a 
child is still hypotonic as a toddler or demonstrates motoric delays, 
then there is likely an underlying neuromuscular abnormality. Hy-
pertonia may be subdivided into spasticity, dystonia, and rigidity.42 
This is never normal and indicates a CNS disorder. Children with 
cerebral palsy are often hypotonic as infants and then develop hyper-
tonia within the first year of life. Hypotonia with associated areflexia 
in a newborn is consistent with spinal muscular atrophy. Acute-on-
set hypotonia and areflexia in a previously healthy child is suggestive 
of botulism or acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. 
T one and reflex exams done on a crying, fussing, or anxious infant 
or child may yield inaccurate results and should be postponed until 
the child is calm. This may take several attempts and may be com-
pleted with the child on the parent’s lap.
Gait assessment requires an understanding of the impact of 
joint mobility, bony alignment, weakness, and tonal abnormali -
ties on gait. Interventions such as bracing, surgery, and medica -
tions will be based on both the examination of muscles at rest and 
during the functional activity of walking. Children’s gait patterns 
change and progress from the age of 1 to approximately 7 years.26 
As their balance and coordination improve, the base of support 
narrows during the gait cycle. The arms progress from a high 
guard position to a mature arm swing. The stance phase progresses 
from a foot flat pattern with an increased foot progression angle 
to a heel-toe progression with the foot in more neutral alignment. 
The stride length increases, and the cadence decreases. Common 
gait deviations are listed in Table 2.7.
Unique factors in the exam of an adolescent patient are the 
impact of growth spurts along with weight changes on joint and 
muscle development, being more prone to trauma and sports-re -
lated injuries, and the possibility of pain that can stem from 
physiologic and/or psychosomatic factors. The clinician should 
screen for conditions such as scoliosis, Osgood-Schlatter disease,

Idiopathic scoliosis commonly presents in the adolescent female 
with a right thoracic curve, although may also be seen in males. 
Neuromuscular scoliosis may develop or worsen with the adoles -
cent growth spurt. Visual inspection of the spine in sitting, stand-

Scoliosis should not be painful. Back pain in the adolescent, espe-
cially adolescents involved in gymnastics or cheerleading, should 
be further evaluated for spondylolisthesis or spondylolysis. Com -
plaints of anterior knee pain or heel pain in the adolescent, espe -

is a common presentation for Osgood-Schlatter disease and Sever 
disease respectively. In Osgood-Schlatter disease, a tight patellar 
tendon causes a traction apophysitis of the anterior tibia. A painful 
bump over the anterior tibial tubercle can be palpated. This is 
commonly seen in boys ages 10- to 15 involved in running or 
jumping sports. Sever disease is a traction apophysitis at the inser-
tion of the Achilles tendon on the calcaneus and also commonly 
seen in athletes involved in running and jumping activities. 
Screening of the adolescent concussion is a common referral. Pro-
tocols for return to play are well established. Please see Chapter 44 
on traumatic brain injury for a further discussion of this topic.
When assessing pain in adolescents, it is best practice to not 
only use standardized pain assessment tools like the visual analog 
scale and do a comprehensive physical exam but also to observe 
nonverbal cues such as guarding or change in facial expression on 
palpation. With adolescent patients, puberty can cause not only 
physical changes but also mental and emotional changes. It is 
imperative to look for signs of self-injury or abuse, such as cuts, 
scratches, or bruises. The clinician should have a higher index of 
suspicion of self-injury if an adolescent is wearing long sleeves or 
long pants in hot weather.
Functional Assessment
There are many tools that can be used to evaluate and quantify 
functional status in the pediatric population. The commonly used 
Functional Independence Measure (FIM) for adults has been 
adapted to children (weeFIM) by adjusting for developmental 
stages. The levels of assistance are the same between the two scales, 
but the expected FIM level for age differs based on developmental 
level (e.g., a 12-month-old would be expected to be at weeFIM 
level 1 [complete dependence] for toileting). This tool has been 
used successfully to measure progress and outcomes for pediatric 
inpatient rehabilitation services as well as in the outpatient setting. 
Developmental skills can be formally assessed with a variety of 
tools ( Table 2.8 ). The Denver Developmental Screening T est 
(DDST-II) is useful as a quick screen to determine whether a child 
is achieving developmental milestones in the areas of gross motor, 
fine motor, language, and personal social skills. 19 It can be used 
with children from birth to 6 years of age. The DDST-II is both 
an observational assessment and an assessment based on parental 
report. If a child fails the Denver screening, a more formalized as-
sessment with the Bayley Scales of Infant and T oddler Develop -
ment3 or the Gesell Developmental Schedules17 is recommended. 
These tests are typically given by therapists. The Bayley test is ap-
propriate for children from birth to 30 months of age, and the 
Gesell test is for children from 4 weeks to 6 years of age. These tests 
are based on direct observation of the child’s skills. Their major 
drawback is that cognitive ability is estimated based on motoric 
performance. In children with significant physical impairment, the 
tests often underestimate cognitive abilities.

ity of Life Inventory evaluates a child’s opinion regarding satisfac-
tion with personal health and wellness. 48 It measures the child’s 
perceived quality of life in physical, emotional, social, and school

Disability-specific assessment tools include the Gross Motor 
Function Measure 66 (GMFM-66), 41 the Manual Ability  
Classification System (MACS), a nd the Quality of Upper Ex -
tremity Skills T est (QUEST) for cerebral palsy. The GMFM-66 
is a subset of the original GMFM-88; it evaluates gross motor 
functions such as lying, rolling, sitting, crawling, standing, walk -
ing, running, and jumping. Individual items are scored on a 
4-point scale ranging from 0 (no initiation) to 4 (completion of 
the task). The MACS and the QUEST both evaluate fine motor 
skills and hand manipulation. The MACS categorizes children 
into one of five levels of function ranging from level 1 (handles 
objects well and easily) to level 5 (unable to handle objects). The 
QUEST evaluates movement patterns and hand function. 10
Special Considerations for Adolescent Patients
Driving is a symbol of independence for adolescents; however, 
it is a great responsibility with many risks that must be addressed. 
Foremost, traffic accidents are the leading cause of death in youth 
in the United States. 8 The clinician can provide education on 
safety and evaluate an adolescent’s readiness to drive. The laws 
governing which medical conditions may prevent someone from 
driving will vary across states and countries. If there are concerns 
about an adolescent’s ability to drive safely, then the clinician 
should consider a referral to occupational therapy for a driver’s 
evaluation program.
T ransition of adolescent care to an adult provider is essential 
for improved clinical outcomes and ensures continuity of devel -
opmental and age-appropriate care. Clinical guidelines recom -
mend that clinicians provide guidance and support to ensure a 
planned transition from pediatric to adult health care, beginning 
at the age of 12 years. 52 Adolescents with intellectual and devel -
opmental disabilities face unique challenges that significantly 
impact their health outcomes, emotional well-being, and quality 
of life. Helping the adolescent and family better advocate for their 
needs is paramount for the clinician. Various tools, from neuro -
psychological testing to individualized career planning, can help 
in future planning for adolescents with disabilities. These can help 
guide the family and clinician in college aspirations, help find ap-
propriate employment, or determine whether the adolescent 
would do better with prolonged high schooling. A day program is 
another option and can improve an adolescent’s quality of life and 
socialization along with providing respite for parents or other 
caregivers. In some situations, it may be best for the parent or 
another trusted adult to gain guardianship when the adolescent 
turns 18 years old. Guardianship, in this context, is a court-or -
dered relationship where one adult (the guardian) is authorized to 
make decisions for and act on behalf of another adult (the ward) 
who is unable to make sound decisions for oneself because of a 
disability. Please note that the court personnel will investigate if a 
less restrictive alternative to guardianship exists, such as power of 
attorney or supported decision making. The clinician can aid in this 
process by assisting with any documentation needed, educating, 
and providing legal resources.
Summary
The pediatric history and physical examination should be tailored to 
each child and family. The child’s developmental level must be 
evaluated and rehabilitation approaches selected and instituted
